speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 3-- interview with Ania Radek. Ania, when we ended the last tape, you were telling me that you were in the investigation room. And the Gestapo had just asked you, where is Chaya. What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told him I don't know. And I was repeating that several times. And I even told him that, being a Christian I don't ever lie. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he started beating me on the head. And he tore my right ear slightly off, here. And kicked me, finally, so hard that the whole chair with me went on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tried to get up. He came again and kicked me. And he got so mad, he started swearing. [SPEAKING GERMAN], and so on. And finally he went to the table and motioned for some other guy to pick me up and take me away. And this other guy took me away. But he locked me in some sort of a-- um-- looked like it was a utility place-- a small-- very small place. And he locked me in there and said goodbye, with a last kick. And I was there. I don't know how long, because I was so half-- I was not quite conscious, then. When I came to, there was another guy who opened the door. And he took me outside. And he was-- He was a Polish-- uh-- some sort of a Polish collaborator. And he led me-- I was asking him-- because I have a loaf of bread with me, that I was carrying, that my aunt gave me, on my-- and I carried that as something very dear-- the last time remnant of my life. And he said, you don't need it, and you will never need any bread. You will never need anything now. You're finished. And I asked him whether I could still go to the washroom and wash up myself-- wash my blood off. So he took me to the washroom, and I washed my blood off. And he took me to the square. There were people already sorted out-- women separated from men. And there were no children anymore. He took me to their women's place. And there were a lot of women. Some really sobbing, crying, and-- I didn't know what was happening, but I knew that it was something terrible happening. And the neighbor next to me told me, they took her children. They took her children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I ask her, where did they take-- where did they take them? And she said, probably to burn. They will burn them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got scared, thinking this woman lost her mind! So we are going to lose our minds, here. I didn't believe that one could burn a child alive-- or whatever that was. And we stood there for the roll call, and they gave us papers-- slip of paper. And they asked us to line up, two by two. And they drove us-- or rather-- I don't call it 'driving,' when you are being escorted-- you're on foot-- to someplace where we had to strip. And men were around-- Gestapo-- looking at us, staring at us. We were very, very terribly not only ashamed but I'd call it violated, in that way. And then we had to give our clothes to-- apparently to delouse it-- to delouse our clothes. And so they put numbers on this-- on the clothes-- and asked us to remember the numbers. And we went into this big place, and we had to wash our heads in some kind of a very burning stuff. There were troughs alongside a wall, and you stood in there. You line up-- everybody was lining up around the troughs. And you had to put your head there. And some women started shrieking, because probably the acid got into their eyes. And, of course, the kapos were going back and forth with their whips and slugging us-- whoever dared to lift their heads from that trough. And, if somebody did or resisted, then that person was punished twice, because the guy would come over and put her head in that water and-- that acid, really-- and keep it longer than usual. So these women lost their hair for ever-- it was burnt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, after that, we were transferred to someplace. I don't know. I was very sick. I was bringing up. I was very sick and hardly conscious.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was this lady. I don't even remember who she was. But she was trying to drag me after her everywhere, so that I would not be left there lying somewhere, and they would drag me away. And we were taken to some camp. And, to this day, I don't you know-- I think from what I have heard, that was a place near Auschwitz. On the 16th of December, we were taken to that place. And we were pushed into this lager-- this-- And it was terrible, because it was extremely cold-- drafty. We were not properly dressed. We were very hungry. We haven't eaten anything. For two days, we were without food, except we had a little bit of something that-- that they would call 'coffee,' which was probably made of acorns. And they packed us in those barracks. And there were women-- women-- the lagerfuherer-- who were taking care of us, telling us what to do and when and where to lie down and what-- and using their power with their own baton. It was a terrible thing. We stayed there, in that-- in those barracks. I believe that was somewhere in-- in February-- February, I believe, 2nd-- something. They took us from those barracks to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were-- all this time that you went from Krakow to these barracks near Auschwitz, and then you went to Dachau, and you were moving with these-- with a group of women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What denomination were the women? Were they Polish, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a variety-- well, from that-- from that camp, in-- I believe this was Brzezinka-- have you-- have you ever heard of Brzezinka? That is vicinity, in the vicinity of Auschwitz. Just almost the same thing, except a bit farther into the-- And they took us from there. When they took us from there, there were several nationalities. I know that they were some French, because I had some notions of French, and I understood some words. There were some French. There were some Jewish people, mostly women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was everybody treated the same? All the women treated the same?

words.text: same? All the women treated the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the same. However, these women seemed to be-- they called them 'shiksa.' I don't know whether you heard that word. 'Shiksa' is the Jewish person who turned-- who became Christian, for one reason or another. And they were taken anyway. And we were taken to Dachau. I remember, in Passau there was a bombardment. And they took us out of the-- of the train-- just to show them that there are people in there, so that they don't bomb, probably, the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were walking there, scared out of our wits, while the sirens were wailing. And then they packed us again back into the-- into the cars and drove us all the way to-- to Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Dachau-- in Dachau, I remember one thing that strikes my memory so vividly. There is this narrow street alongside this very long, long train of barbed-wire fences. And there are some poplars-- young poplars. And they look so desolate, because everything is lifeless, of course, in December-- in February, I mean. And they took us there, also again, on the roll call, inside. And we stood there for quite a long while, tired, some people who were falling. Some people were almost dying, and some people were disposed of. We didn't care. We were so tired and worn-out that everybody probably wished to be dead. And then, finally, they drove us into one of the barracks. And there we stayed for quite a while. Or stayed till 9th of-- I think the 9th or 10th of December, in '42. And in '42--

speaker: interviewer
text: In between, what were we doing? Quite a lot of women were taken out, chosen at random from the-- from the barrack, to do certain services to work around the kitchen and around the premises, cleaning. And I-- What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was mostly cleaning a place where the German-- the Gestapo-- were having their suppers or dinners. And, after everything was cleaned, we were cleaning the floors-- washing the floors. We were washing even the walls and the doors. And then, around that barrack-- 'barrack'-- the building, rather, because this I would not call a 'barrack.' It was a cement building, it's-- And sometimes you were taken to the kitchen, to watch the kitchen. Around the kitchen they were throwing the-- the-- all kinds of filth outside. And if they didn't throw it around-- so I had to clean it around-- the-- the-- the mess around there. We always had some different kind of chores to do. And at times we were, of course-- whenever we did something not to their liking or being just simply exhausted-- too exhausted to do it, and we just started hesitating, you know-- resting a little bit. So the-- the Gestapo-- the kapo would come with the-- with the whip and whip us up, and, or push us in-- I remember I was once pushed right into the wall. Because I stood in front of the wall, thinking I was supposed to clean that wall that was full of filth. Somebody was probably tortured against that wall. There was blood and dirt and feces. And I was to clean that. And I was hesitating, standing there, and that man came right there to me, and he banged me right into the wall, into that-- into that dirt. And-- and my face was bleeding and soiled. And I just couldn't just come to myself. And I was excusing myself and begging-- in Polish, of course. I didn't know my German. And, of course, that got on his nerves. He even pushed me again, and so on and that. So finally I cleaned that wall. And I remember I came-- came back to the barrack. And I was bringing up, and I was very, very upset and quite a-- this is one of many incidents that we had. And quite everybody, every day, brought some-- some account of terrible story-- terrible happening to her, in that place. And what really struck me was the great friendship and solidarity between those people. Somebody got hurt, the other women would come and would console-- would [INAUDIBLE] next to you. The Jewish people-- the Jewish women were in a different barrack. Because they sorted us out when we came, as you know, for the roll call. And also I remember-- also, I forgot to tell you that, when we came first there we had-- we were all shaven. And we were given this silly kerchiefs everybody wear-- had to wear. And I remember my-- the friend from-- from-- from the trip-- she had this very beautiful long hair, down to her [INAUDIBLE]-- lower back. And she was so desperate. She wanted to take her life. And she was so upset about having her hair shaven. She was-- she was so proud-- probably very proud of it. And quite a lot of women were in a terrible, terrible predicament.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with the women in the other-- in the other barracks-- the Jewish women?

speaker: interviewee
text: We only had-- the only contact we had-- when we were going for our soup-- for our soup-- daily food, and when our lineup met their lineup-- like, the barrack that their barracks was following-- and they were just joining our lineup, then we were talking. And the ladies there were-- most of them were very emaciated. Very, very-- almost finished. Very lean, with hardly any-- any fat on them. Just bones-- sheer bones sticking out. I believe they had been there for a longer period of time. And they could hardly talk. When they were saying something, it was so quiet that you would have to strain your ears to-- to listen to them. And quite a lot of them-- they were so courageous, in spite of it all. I noticed that one helped the other, and-- and they were saying-- I heard-- overheard-- the were of Polish origin-- they were saying, for example, [SPEAKING POLISH]. That means, [INAUDIBLE], wait-- wait. You will-- you will-- you will survive. You will manage. You will manage, somehow. And they were giving each other the courage. The thing was that, later-- the later-- the later, towards our-- towards my going away from that camp, I noticed that that lineup was shorter and shorter and shorter. And then there was another influx of new women. And what happened to them, I didn't know. Because I wasn't even aware-- strange as it may be. I was sort of-- I was probably in denial of it all. I didn't know that they were being burnt-- that they were being cremated. And they were being probably used some kind of gas on them. And-- and, of course, thousands of them went to the inferno there. It-- it hurt beyond human endurance. And there was-- at that time, there was no race-- no conception of whether you were French or Gypsy or Polish or Jewish. There was no-- everybody was in the same-- in the same-- hell-- that's what I'd call it. It was hell. And all you could do is just stare ahead of you. We were so dehumanized that, all through life, you never will regain your humanity. You will never feel that you're somebody or something. A lot of people, even though they went out of the concentration camp-- they simply deteriorated. They either got very sick. They got very, very negative in their lives, [INAUDIBLE]. And they were-- most of them-- I don't know, but they suffered terrible depression-- bouts of depression. You cannot get over it. I myself, I have been treated for depression for many, many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ania, for how long were you in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Dachau till December-- December of '42. And then there was this group of men. I don't know. They looked like businessmen. They came over. And our lagerfuherin-- the lager lady-- came, and she called some people that was her whim, I believe. And she called aloud. We lined up outside. And the men were just staring at us, like you would stare at a horse that you are going to buy. And touching our muscles, our hands, and-- and even asked to show our teeth to them, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, finally, they chose certain-- a group of younger people. Were you amongst that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was amongst that group.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us on the truck. There was a big truck that came. And they transported us to Augsburg, to Hans Deuter camp. There-- there were already other people there-- other women. It was a place underground-- a camp that was underground, which was supervised and watched by guards. And locked, always, for the night. During the day, we were escorted to the factory, where I was sewing the-- what do you call that-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- covers for the trucks-- tarps. And it was a very heavy thing-- heavy job. In the beginning, I could hardly carry anything, so they put me on the sewing machine. And I managed to catch up that work soon-- very soon. And I worked on that machine-- heavy-duty machine, sewing those tarps all day. And we had a very, very exhausting kind of life, and the food was very poor, so we probably got around-- somewhere around 900 to 1,100, 1,000 calories-- something like that. Mostly soup made of squash. Or sometimes we got potatoes. And there was this Ukrainian woman that was supposed to distribute the food. She was bringing, for example, a big vat of potatoes. Some of the potatoes were rotten. So she gives them anyway, and she gives them sometimes because she doesn't like this person, so she gives her a rotten potato. And he had fights. We had-- we had all kinds of terrible stories there. We had this big dormitory, divided by a long, long table-- series of tables put together. And on one side were Ukrainians, who were treated with special, special favors. Like, they could go out on town. They could go to the washrooms whenever they wanted. While we, the Polish girls, were treated like terribly-- like we were treated in other camps. And there were fights and insults flying across that table. And we had a lot of strange happenings there that only probably my book will tell. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long were you in Hans Deuter?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Hans Deuter till 1944. And 1944, in February, there was a huge bombardment of Augsburg. We were taken-- each time there was a bombardment, we were taken to the bunkers, or shelters. And during this bombardment, the shelter on one side was-- uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to go to a break now.
